annotations.label: Prime number (lead)

anchor.snippet: A prime number (or a prime ) is a natural number greater than 1 that is not a product of two smaller natural numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime` is defined as a natural number ≥ 2 not expressible as a product of smaller naturals.

annotations.label: Composite number

anchor.snippet: A natural number greater than 1 that is not prime is called a composite number

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `Composite` predicate; only `¬ Nat.Prime n` is used.

annotations.label: 5 is prime, 4 is composite

anchor.snippet: 5 is prime because the only ways of writing it as a product

annotations.note: Concrete primality facts like `Nat.prime_two`, `Nat.prime_three`, `Nat.prime_five` are present and `norm_num`'s `Prime` extension decides such examples.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic (lead)

anchor.snippet: every natural number greater than 1 is either a prime itself or can be factorized as a product of primes that is unique up to their order

annotations.note: Existence and uniqueness of the prime factorization of naturals are formalized via `Nat.factors` and `UniqueFactorizationMonoid.factors_unique`.

annotations.label: Prime number

anchor.snippet: is called a prime number (or a prime ) if it is greater than 1 and cannot be written as the product of two smaller natural numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime` is the standard predicate, equivalent to `Irreducible` on ℕ.

annotations.label: Prime via divisors

anchor.snippet: they are the numbers with exactly two positive divisors

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Nat.Prime` is defined via irreducibility, and the divisor-count characterization is implicit (e.g., `Nat.divisors_prime_pow`/`Nat.Prime.divisors`-style lemmas) rather than the literal definition.

annotations.label: Primes among 1 through 6

anchor.snippet: among the numbers 1 through 6, the numbers 2, 3, and 5 are the prime numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.prime_two`, `Nat.prime_three`, `Nat.prime_five` and decidability of `Nat.Prime` (via `norm_num`) cover these examples.

annotations.label: First 25 prime numbers

anchor.snippet: The first 25 prime numbers (all the prime numbers less than 100)

annotations.note: `Nat.primesBelow 100` computes the finite set of primes below 100, but the explicit enumeration of the first 25 primes is not a named theorem.

annotations.label: Every prime > 2 is odd

anchor.snippet: every prime number other than 2 is an odd number

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime.eq_two_or_odd'` proves that every prime equals 2 or is odd.

annotations.label: Wilson's theorem (history)

anchor.snippet: Wilson's theorem , characterizing the prime numbers as the numbers

annotations.note: Wilson's theorem (`Nat.prime_iff_fac_equiv_neg_one`) characterizes primes via `(n-1)! ≡ -1 (mod n)`.

annotations.label: Fermat numbers

anchor.snippet: Fermat also investigated the primality of the Fermat numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.fermatNumber n = 2^(2^n) + 1` is defined in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.Fermat`.

annotations.label: Mersenne primes

anchor.snippet: Marin Mersenne studied the Mersenne primes, prime numbers of the form

annotations.note: `mersenne p = 2^p - 1` is defined and primality is studied via `lucas_lehmer_sufficiency`.

annotations.label: Goldbach's conjecture (history)

anchor.snippet: Christian Goldbach formulated Goldbach's conjecture , that every even number is the sum of two primes

annotations.note: Goldbach's conjecture on sums of two primes is not stated in Mathlib (only Goldbach's coprimality theorem for Fermat numbers appears).

annotations.label: Bertrand's postulate

anchor.snippet: A weaker consequence of this high density of primes was Bertrand's postulate

annotations.note: Bertrand's postulate is proved in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.Bertrand`.

annotations.label: Prime number theorem (history)

anchor.snippet: the result is now known as the prime number theorem

annotations.note: The full PNT is not yet in Mathlib; only Chebyshev-type asymptotic bounds on π and the related ψ/θ functions are formalized.

annotations.label: Dirichlet's theorem on arithmetic progressions (history)

anchor.snippet: Dirichlet's theorem on arithmetic progressions , that certain arithmetic progressions contain infinitely many primes

annotations.note: Dirichlet's theorem on primes in arithmetic progressions is proved in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.LSeries.PrimesInAP`.

annotations.label: Green–Tao theorem (history)

anchor.snippet: the Green–Tao theorem (2004) that there are arbitrarily long arithmetic progressions of prime numbers

annotations.note: The Green–Tao theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Zhang's bounded prime gaps

anchor.snippet: Yitang Zhang 's 2013 proof that there exist infinitely many prime gaps of bounded size

annotations.note: Zhang's bounded gaps result is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prime factorization

anchor.snippet: Writing a number as a product of prime numbers is called a prime factorization of the number

annotations.note: `Nat.factorization` gives a number's prime factorization as a finsupp; `Nat.primeFactorsList` provides the list of prime factors.

annotations.label: Prime factors

anchor.snippet: The terms in the product are called prime factors

annotations.note: `Nat.primeFactors` and `Nat.primeFactorsList` give the set/list of prime factors of a natural number.

annotations.label: Fundamental theorem of arithmetic

anchor.snippet: This theorem states that every integer larger than 1 can be written as a product of one or more primes

annotations.note: Existence/uniqueness of prime factorization in ℕ follows from `UniqueFactorizationMonoid` infrastructure; Mathlib explicitly proves `Nat.primeFactorsList_unique` etc.

annotations.label: Euclid's lemma

anchor.snippet: Some proofs of the uniqueness of prime factorizations are based on Euclid's lemma

annotations.note: Euclid's lemma is `Nat.Prime.dvd_mul` (and `Prime.dvd_or_dvd` in general commutative monoids).

annotations.label: Euclid's theorem (infinitude of primes)

anchor.snippet: There are infinitely many prime numbers

annotations.note: `Nat.exists_infinite_primes` (and `Nat.infinite_setOf_prime`) state the infinitude of primes.

annotations.label: Euclid numbers

anchor.snippet: The numbers formed by adding one to the products of the smallest primes are called Euclid numbers

annotations.note: Euclid numbers (primorial + 1) are not named in Mathlib, though `Nat.primorial` exists.

annotations.label: Sixth Euclid number is composite

anchor.snippet: The first five of them are prime, but the sixth

annotations.note: This specific numerical fact about Euclid numbers is not recorded in Mathlib.

annotations.label: No polynomial formula for primes

anchor.snippet: there is no non-constant polynomial , even in several variables, that takes only prime values

annotations.note: No Mathlib statement asserts the non-existence of a prime-valued polynomial.

annotations.label: Mills' theorem and Wright's theorem

anchor.snippet: Other examples of prime-generating formulas come from Mills' theorem and a theorem of Wright

annotations.note: Mills' constant/theorem and Wright's theorem are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Goldbach's conjecture

anchor.snippet: Goldbach's conjecture , which asserts that every even integer

annotations.note: Goldbach's conjecture is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Vinogradov's theorem

anchor.snippet: Vinogradov's theorem says that every sufficiently large odd integer can be written as a sum of three primes

annotations.note: Vinogradov's three-primes theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Chen's theorem

anchor.snippet: Chen's theorem says that every sufficiently large even number can be expressed as the sum of a prime and a semiprime

annotations.note: Chen's theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Existence of arbitrarily large prime gaps

anchor.snippet: The existence of arbitrarily large prime gaps can be seen by noting that the sequence

annotations.note: The statement that arbitrarily large prime gaps exist is not a named theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Twin prime conjecture

anchor.snippet: It is conjectured that there are infinitely many twin primes

annotations.note: The twin prime conjecture is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polignac's conjecture

anchor.snippet: Polignac's conjecture states more generally that for every positive integer

annotations.note: Polignac's conjecture is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: First Hardy–Littlewood conjecture

anchor.snippet: Their infinitude and density are the subject of the first Hardy–Littlewood conjecture

annotations.note: The first Hardy–Littlewood conjecture is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euler's solution to the Basel problem

anchor.snippet: the solution to the Basel problem

annotations.note: `hasSum_zeta_two` proves the Basel problem `∑ 1/n² = π²/6`.

annotations.label: Probability two random numbers are coprime

anchor.snippet: is the limiting probability that two random numbers selected uniformly from a large range are relatively prime

annotations.note: The density result that the probability two random integers are coprime equals 6/π² is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Divergence of sum of reciprocals of primes

anchor.snippet: Euler showed that, for any arbitrary real number

annotations.note: `not_summable_one_div_on_primes` shows the sum of reciprocals of primes diverges.

annotations.label: Mertens' second theorem

anchor.snippet: The growth rate of this sum is described more precisely by Mertens' second theorem

annotations.note: Mertens' second theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Brun's theorem

anchor.snippet: Brun's theorem states that the sum of the reciprocals of twin primes

annotations.note: Brun's theorem on the convergence of the sum of reciprocals of twin primes is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prime-counting function

anchor.section: Number of primes below a given bound
anchor.snippet: is defined as the number of primes not greater than

annotations.note: `Nat.primeCounting` is the standard π(n) function.

annotations.label: Prime number theorem

anchor.section: Number of primes below a given bound
anchor.snippet: The prime number theorem states that

annotations.note: The asymptotic statement π(n) ~ n/log n is not yet formalized in Mathlib; only Chebyshev-type bounds appear.

annotations.label: Offset logarithmic integral estimate

anchor.section: Number of primes below a given bound
anchor.snippet: is given by the offset logarithmic integral

annotations.note: The offset logarithmic integral and its asymptotic for π(n) are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Arithmetic progression

anchor.snippet: An arithmetic progression is a finite or infinite sequence of numbers such that consecutive numbers in the sequence all have the same difference

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `ArithmeticProgression` type; APs are encoded via `fun n ↦ a + n • d` ad hoc (e.g., in `Combinatorics.Additive.AP`).

annotations.label: Dirichlet's theorem on arithmetic progressions

anchor.snippet: Dirichlet's theorem on arithmetic progressions asserts that the progression contains infinitely many primes

annotations.note: Dirichlet's theorem on infinitely many primes in arithmetic progressions is in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.LSeries.PrimesInAP`.

annotations.label: Green–Tao theorem

anchor.snippet: The Green–Tao theorem shows that there are arbitrarily long finite arithmetic progressions consisting only of primes

annotations.note: The Green–Tao theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Euler's prime-generating polynomial

anchor.snippet: Euler noted that the function

annotations.note: Euler's polynomial n² − n + 41 and its prime-generating property are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hardy–Littlewood conjecture F

anchor.snippet: The Hardy–Littlewood conjecture F predicts the density of primes among the values of quadratic polynomials

annotations.note: Hardy–Littlewood conjecture F is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Riemann hypothesis (zeros of zeta)

anchor.section: Zeta function and the Riemann hypothesis
anchor.snippet: the Riemann hypothesis , which asks where the zeros of the Riemann zeta function

annotations.note: `RiemannHypothesis` is stated as a `Prop` in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.LSeries.RiemannZeta`.

anchors.section: Zeta function and the Riemann hypothesis
anchors.snippet: the Riemann hypothesis , which asks where the zeros of the Riemann zeta function

annotations.label: Euler product

anchor.section: Zeta function and the Riemann hypothesis
anchor.snippet: This equality between a sum and a product, discovered by Euler, is called an Euler product

annotations.note: Euler product identities are in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.EulerProduct.Basic`.

anchors.section: Zeta function and the Riemann hypothesis
anchors.snippet: This equality between a sum and a product, discovered by Euler, is called an Euler product

annotations.label: Riemann hypothesis (statement)

anchor.section: Zeta function and the Riemann hypothesis
anchor.snippet: The Riemann hypothesis states that the zeros of the zeta-function are all either negative even numbers

annotations.note: `RiemannHypothesis` formalizes the statement about the location of zeta zeros.

annotations.label: Modular arithmetic

anchor.snippet: Modular arithmetic modifies usual arithmetic by only using the numbers

annotations.note: `ZMod n` is the standard formalization of modular arithmetic.

annotations.label: Modular arithmetic is a field iff modulus is prime

anchor.snippet: division by all nonzero numbers is possible if and only if the modulus is prime

annotations.note: `ZMod p` is given a `Field` instance when `p` is prime.

annotations.label: Fermat's little theorem

anchor.snippet: Fermat's little theorem states that if

annotations.note: `ZMod.pow_card` (and `ZMod.pow_card_sub_one_eq_one`) state Fermat's little theorem.

annotations.label: Giuga's conjecture

anchor.snippet: Giuga's conjecture says that this equation is also a sufficient condition

annotations.note: Giuga's conjecture is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wilson's theorem

anchor.snippet: Wilson's theorem says that an integer

annotations.note: Wilson's theorem is `Nat.prime_iff_fac_equiv_neg_one`.

annotations.label: p-adic order

anchor.snippet: is the number of copies of

annotations.note: `padicValNat p n` and `padicValRat` formalize the p-adic order/valuation.

annotations.label: Ostrowski's theorem

anchor.snippet: According to Ostrowski's theorem

annotations.note: Ostrowski's theorem for ℚ is proved in `Mathlib.NumberTheory.Ostrowski`.

annotations.label: Local–global principle

anchor.snippet: The local–global principle allows certain problems over the rational numbers to be solved by piecing together solutions from each of their places

annotations.note: The Hasse–Minkowski local–global principle is not stated as a theorem in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Prime element of a ring

anchor.snippet: is called prime if it is nonzero, has no multiplicative inverse

annotations.note: `Prime` in a commutative monoid with zero matches the ring-theoretic definition.

annotations.label: Irreducible element

anchor.snippet: An element is irreducible if it is neither a unit nor the product of two other non-unit elements

annotations.note: `Irreducible` is the standard predicate matching the Wikipedia definition.

annotations.label: Prime elements are irreducible

anchor.snippet: In an arbitrary ring, all prime elements are irreducible

annotations.note: `Prime.irreducible` proves every prime element is irreducible.

annotations.label: Fermat's theorem on sums of two squares

anchor.snippet: Fermat's theorem on sums of two squares , which states that an odd prime

annotations.note: `Nat.Prime.sq_add_sq` shows any prime with p % 4 ≠ 3 is a sum of two squares.

annotations.label: Prime ideal

anchor.snippet: Prime ideals , which generalize prime elements in the sense that

annotations.note: `Ideal.IsPrime` is the standard prime-ideal predicate.

annotations.label: Lasker–Noether theorem

anchor.snippet: The fundamental theorem of arithmetic generalizes to the Lasker–Noether theorem

annotations.note: `Submodule.isLasker` formalizes Lasker–Noether: every submodule in a Noetherian module admits a primary decomposition.

annotations.label: Spectrum of a ring

anchor.snippet: The spectrum of a ring is a geometric space whose points are the prime ideals of the ring

annotations.note: `PrimeSpectrum R` is defined as the type of prime ideals of `R`.

annotations.label: Chebotarev's density theorem

anchor.snippet: is addressed by Chebotarev's density theorem

annotations.note: Chebotarev's density theorem is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sylow theorems

anchor.snippet: the Sylow theorems imply that

annotations.note: The Sylow theorems are formalized in `Mathlib.GroupTheory.Sylow`.

annotations.label: Lagrange's theorem (prime order groups)

anchor.snippet: By Lagrange's theorem , any group of prime order is a cyclic group

annotations.note: `isCyclic_of_prime_card` shows any finite group of prime order is cyclic.

annotations.label: Burnside's theorem (two-prime order solvable)

anchor.snippet: by Burnside's theorem any group whose order is divisible by only two primes is solvable

annotations.note: Burnside's p^a q^b theorem on solvability is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Trial division

anchor.snippet: is called trial division

annotations.note: `Nat.minFac` finds the smallest prime factor by trial division; this serves as Mathlib's primality algorithm but `trial division` is not named.

annotations.label: Trial division on 37

anchor.snippet: to check whether 37 is prime

annotations.note: The specific worked example for 37 is not in Mathlib (though `Nat.Prime 37` is decidable via `norm_num`).

annotations.label: Sieve of Eratosthenes

anchor.snippet: The oldest known method for generating a list of primes is called the sieve of Eratosthenes

annotations.note: The sieve of Eratosthenes is not implemented as a named algorithm in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sieve of Atkin

anchor.snippet: Another more asymptotically efficient sieving method for the same problem is the sieve of Atkin

annotations.note: The sieve of Atkin is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solovay–Strassen primality test

anchor.snippet: the Solovay–Strassen primality test on a given number

annotations.note: The Solovay–Strassen primality test is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pseudoprime

anchor.snippet: A composite number that passes such a test is called a pseudoprime

annotations.note: `Nat.FermatPsp` formalizes Fermat pseudoprimes.

annotations.label: AKS primality test

anchor.snippet: such as the AKS primality test

annotations.note: The AKS primality test is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Elliptic curve primality proving

anchor.snippet: such as some variations of elliptic curve primality proving

annotations.note: Elliptic curve primality proving (ECPP) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lucas–Lehmer primality test

anchor.section: Special-purpose algorithms and the largest known prime
anchor.snippet: the Lucas–Lehmer primality test can determine whether a Mersenne number

annotations.note: `lucas_lehmer_sufficiency` proves the Lucas–Lehmer test correctly certifies Mersenne primes.

annotations.label: Infinitely many Mersenne primes conjecture

anchor.section: Special-purpose algorithms and the largest known prime
anchor.snippet: It is conjectured that there are infinitely many Mersenne primes

annotations.note: The conjecture on infinitely many Mersenne primes is not stated in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Integer factorization

anchor.snippet: the task of providing one (or all) prime factors is referred to as factorization of

annotations.note: `Nat.primeFactorsList` computes the list of prime factors; `Nat.factorization` gives the multiset.

annotations.label: Shor's algorithm

anchor.snippet: Shor's algorithm can factor any integer in a polynomial number of steps on a quantum computer

annotations.note: Shor's quantum factoring algorithm is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Eisenstein's criterion

anchor.snippet: Eisenstein's criterion , a test for whether a polynomial is irreducible based on divisibility of its coefficients by a prime number

annotations.note: `Polynomial.irreducible_of_eisenstein_criterion` is the classical Eisenstein irreducibility criterion.

annotations.label: Fermat primes

anchor.snippet: Fermat primes are primes of the form

annotations.note: `Nat.fermatNumber` is defined but there is no dedicated `FermatPrime` predicate; one can write `(Nat.fermatNumber n).Prime`.

annotations.label: Gauss–Wantzel theorem on regular polygon constructibility

anchor.snippet: is constructible using straightedge and compass if and only if the odd prime factors of

annotations.note: The Gauss–Wantzel theorem characterizing constructible regular polygons is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equal-area equal-perimeter partition of convex polygon

anchor.snippet: It is possible to partition any convex polygon into

annotations.note: This combinatorial-geometric partition theorem (related to prime powers) is not in Mathlib.
